title: refundpilot (ADR-0007 ↔ code)
scope: handle_refund in refund_agent.py against ADR-0007 (refund auto-approval cap). Pinned with `invairiant collect --scope adr --path docs/adr/0007-refund-caps.md`; in scope = the code the ADR references (refund_agent.py, payments.py).

lens_scores.verdict: the cap the ADR calls the enforced boundary is observed but never enforced (RFP-001)
untrusted model output reaches issue_payout unbounded; no server-side cap behind it (RFP-001)
control flow terminates and is simple; the defect is a missing gate, not a loop
the safety control exists on paper (a warning log) but does not stop the unsafe action (RFP-001)

findings.claim: The $50 auto-refund cap is checked but not enforced: handle_refund logs a warning when the model's amount exceeds MAX_AUTO_REFUND_USD, then issues the payout unconditionally. The agent can autonomously pay out any amount the model returns — the exact boundary ADR-0007 says must gate the executing path.

evidence.description: `if rec["amount"] > MAX_AUTO_REFUND_USD: log.warning(...)` — the over-cap branch only logs; no return, no human-routing
`issue_payout(order.customer_id, rec["amount"])` executes on the next line for any amount — the cap does not gate it
"There is NO server-side cap — the caller is trusted": handle_refund is the only enforcement point, as ADR-0007 states
ADR-0007: "above the cap MUST be routed to a human — the agent must not issue it autonomously." The code warns and issues it.

findings.risk: A prompt injection in order data, or an ordinary model hallucination, yields an arbitrary `amount` that is paid out with no cap and no human — direct, unbounded loss of customer funds. The warning log makes it look handled in review and in dashboards.
findings.recommendation: Gate issue_payout on the cap: for amount > MAX_AUTO_REFUND_USD, route to human review and return — do not call issue_payout. Add a test that a $5000 model-approved refund does NOT pay out. Treat the model `amount` as advisory, the cap as the enforced bound.

verification.method: read refund_agent.py:24-33 control flow — confirmed no return/raise between the cap warning (29-30) and issue_payout (33); confirmed payments.py has no cap; cross-read ADR-0007
verification.notes: the branch on the cap is a warning, not a gate

observations.text: llm.py returns the model's JSON parsed and trusted as-is; the whole design treats an advisory, injectable input as authority on the money-moving path.

hypotheses.text: Prompt injection via order data could exfiltrate customer PII into the model prompt.
hypotheses.rejected_reason: the prompt at refund_agent.py:20-22 interpolates only order.id and order.total — no customer PII or free-text notes are read into it. No evidence, so not a finding.

summary.executive_summary: One S1 fails the gate: refundpilot's refund agent does not enforce the $50 auto-approval cap that ADR-0007 calls a hard boundary — it warns over-cap refunds and pays them out anyway (RFP-001). Control flow is otherwise simple and terminating; the defect is a missing gate, not complexity. A normal review sees a cap check and a warning and passes it; the oracle-boundary lens verified the check does not gate the action.
summary.weakest_reason: the ADR's enforced boundary is not enforced — exactly what this lens exists to catch (RFP-001)

required_actions.action: Gate issue_payout on MAX_AUTO_REFUND_USD; route over-cap refunds to a human and return; add a 'model-approved $5000 refund does not pay out' test
